local_names.romanized_local: Bai hujiao fen (Mandarin); Tieu trang xay (Vietnamese romanized)

region_of_origin: Southeast Asia (historically Vietnam, Indonesia); widely used globally

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code refers to white pepper as a substance; ground form is the closest available match.

main_ingredients: white peppercorns (Piper nigrum, outer husk removed)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of ground white pepper is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 2.4 g), used as a seasoning in cooking or at the table.

nutrient_source.title: White pepper, spices — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for white pepper, ground. Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 02034 (Spices, pepper, white) for validation and supplementary micronutrient fields; values are consistent.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Spices — Analogous estimate based on low-GI spice category; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ground white pepper in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on the analogous category of dried ground spices with high dietary fibre content (26.2 g/100g) and complex carbohydrate matrix. At a typical serving of 2.4 g, the glycemic load is negligible (GL approximately 0.5). This is consistent with the established principle that high-fibre spices used in small culinary quantities have minimal glycemic impact. Estimation basis documented per Project Veritas v2 source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium content (5 mg/100g); typical serving contributes less than 0.1 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Ground white pepper is safe for individuals with hypertension; at a typical culinary dose of 1–2 g per serving, sodium contribution is essentially zero, and the high dietary fibre (26.2 g/100g) and calcium (265 mg/100g) content may offer modest cardiovascular benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible per-serving carbohydrate load; piperine may improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although white pepper contains 68.6 g carbohydrate per 100g, typical culinary servings of 1–2 g contribute less than 1.4 g carbohydrate, making glycemic impact negligible; piperine, the active alkaloid in white pepper, has demonstrated evidence for improving insulin sensitivity and glucose metabolism in preclinical studies, though individuals on diabetes medications should consult their pharmacist as piperine may enhance drug absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat; high dietary fibre; piperine has antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ground white pepper is appropriate for individuals with cardiovascular disease; it is very low in saturated fat (0.65 g/100g) and sodium (5 mg/100g), and its high dietary fibre content (26.2 g/100g) supports lipid management; piperine has demonstrated antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity that may confer additional cardiovascular benefit at regular culinary use.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Piperine inhibits NF-κB inflammatory pathways; anti-inflammatory alkaloid
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: White pepper contains piperine, an anti-inflammatory alkaloid with evidence for inhibiting NF-κB inflammatory pathways and reducing joint inflammation; it may be used freely as a culinary spice, though excessive quantities may irritate the gastrointestinal tract in patients on NSAIDs.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ground white pepper is a finely milled spice produced from dried white peppercorns (Piper nigrum), which are peppercorns that have had their outer dark husk removed before or after drying, leaving the inner seed. The result is a pale, off-white to cream-coloured fine powder with a sharp, hot, and slightly fermented or musty spicy flavour that is more pungent and less complex than black pepper. It is widely used in European, Chinese, Southeast Asian, Vietnamese, and Singaporean cuisines as a table condiment and cooking spice, particularly in white sauces, soups, and dishes where dark pepper specks are aesthetically undesirable. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 296 kcal, 10.4 g protein, 2.12 g fat, 68.61 g carbohydrate, and a notably high dietary fibre content of 26.2 g. Sodium is very low at 5 mg/100g. Calcium is high at 265 mg/100g and iron at 14.31 mg/100g, though these are not nutritionally significant at typical culinary serving sizes of 1–2 g. The active compound piperine has demonstrated anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and insulin-sensitising properties in research settings. Clinically, white pepper is suitable for individuals with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease when used in normal culinary quantities, as per-serving contributions of sodium, carbohydrate, and fat are negligible. Piperine may enhance the bioavailability of co-administered nutrients and medications, which is relevant for patients on polypharmacy.
common_user_queries: Is white pepper good for diabetes?
White pepper vs black pepper nutrition
How much sodium in white pepper?
Can I use white pepper if I have high blood pressure?
What is piperine in white pepper?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved source for Singapore food records) with cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 02034 (Spices, pepper, white). Values are consistent between sources. GI is an analogous estimate documented per policy.
notes: trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace for dried ground spice per USDA SR Legacy entry 02034); cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-derived spice contains no cholesterol); vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-derived food); vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — dried ground spice with no UV exposure pathway). vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to -1: this field is not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry and is listed as not available in USDA SR Legacy entry 02034 for white pepper. folate_mcg_dfe set to -1: folate data for white pepper is not consistently reported in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy entry 02034; data unavailable in both approved sources consulted. vitamin_e_mg set to -1: vitamin E data for ground white pepper is not reported in HPB Foodo and is absent from USDA SR Legacy entry 02034 for this specific spice. selenium_mcg set to -1: selenium data for ground white pepper is not reported in HPB Foodo and is not available in USDA SR Legacy entry 02034. saturated_fat_g (0.65), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.74), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.62), sugar_g (0.64), vitamin_c_mg (21.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.09), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.18), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (1.01), vitamin_b6_mg (0.1), phosphorus_mg (176.0), magnesium_mg (90.0), zinc_mg (1.14), water_g (11.42) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 02034 (Spices, pepper, white) as supplementary data where HPB did not report these fields. GI is an analogous estimate (approximately 30, low category) as no direct GI measurement exists for ground white pepper in the University of Sydney GI Database; GL per 2.4 g serving is approximately 0.5. Dietary tag 'wholegrain' from input removed as ground white pepper is a spice, not a wholegrain product. 'high_fibre' and 'low_sodium' dietary tags retained as accurate. Vietnamese local name 'Tiêu trắng xay' added to satisfy multilingual coverage; white pepper is a historically significant crop in Vietnam (Phu Quoc island) and the Vietnamese name is well-established.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Record for White pepper, ground (F10060049) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard after three iterations. Key fixes in iteration 3: (1) Vietnamese local name 'Tiêu trắng xay' added to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance and Q1/Q8 requirements; (2) G3 numeric hygiene fully resolved by explicitly documenting each -1 field (vitamin_a_mcg_rae, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with specific unavailability reasons and approved source citations in notes. Nutrient data sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary) with USDA SR Legacy entry 02034 cross-validation. GI is an analogous estimate (30, low) documented per policy. Ontology anchors are defensible and honest. All aliases are internally consistent between flat array and structured object.
